sub_problems.description: Quantify the current backend's performance, reliability, and maintenance costs
constraints: Metrics must be comparable to potential Rust implementation

sub_problems.description: Evaluate Rust's technical suitability for the specific backend workload
constraints: Evaluation must consider both current and anticipated future requirements

sub_problems.description: Calculate the economic impact of rewriting vs. incremental improvement
constraints: Must account for both direct costs and opportunity costs

sub_problems.description: Assess organizational readiness and risk tolerance
constraints: Must consider both technical and human factors

assumptions.text: The current backend has measurable deficiencies that justify considering a rewrite
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: This is the core premise but hasn't been verified with data

assumptions.text: Rust's technical advantages (memory safety, performance) are relevant to this specific backend
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: Depends on workload characteristics which may not align with Rust's strengths

assumptions.text: The organization has sufficient resources to undertake a major rewrite without jeopardizing core business
assumptions.label: UNKNOWN

assumptions.text: The current backend's architecture can be successfully translated to Rust
assumptions.label: UNKNOWN

assumptions.text: The benefits of a rewrite outweigh the disruption to feature development
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: This is the central value proposition but requires validation

failure_modes: Underestimating the 'unknown unknowns' in the current system that only surface during rewrite
Failing to account for the combinatorial complexity of edge cases in the existing system
Over-optimism about team's ability to become productive in Rust while maintaining the old system
Neglecting the 'second system effect' where the rewrite becomes over-engineered
Misjudging the business opportunity cost of delayed features during the transition period

candidates.content: The entire problem framing assumes a rational decision-making process that rarely exists in real organizations. SP1's quantification requirement is fundamentally flawed — current metrics are measured within existing architectural constraints, making them incomparable to a Rust implementation that would inevitably require architectural changes. The 'representative measurement period' constraint is meaningless when the system being measured will be replaced. SP2's technical evaluation ignores the reality that Rust's borrow checker and ownership model fundamentally change how systems are designed, making 'translation' impossible — you're not translating, you're redesigning. The assumption that libraries/frameworks can be mapped 1:1 is dangerously naive; Rust's ecosystem forces different architectural patterns. SP3's economic analysis is pure fiction — development timelines for rewrites are consistently underestimated by 2-3x, and 'business opportunity costs' are impossible to quantify when you don't know what opportunities will emerge during the rewrite period. SP4's organizational assessment fails to account for the psychological toll of maintaining legacy systems while building new ones, which consistently leads to talent attrition. The most critical hidden failure: every assumption treats this as a technical decision when it's actually a political one. The 'measurable deficiencies' hypothesis is often just engineering frustration disguised as objective analysis. The 'sufficient resources' unknown is actually a certainty — no organization ever has sufficient resources for both rewriting and maintaining business momentum simultaneously.
key_insights: The comparison is invalid because you cannot compare metrics from a system designed with one paradigm to a hypothetical system designed with another — you're comparing apples to theoretical oranges.
Rust's safety guarantees come at the cost of development velocity, particularly for backend systems where memory safety is often less critical than rapid iteration and business logic changes.

The rewrite decision creates an immediate two-class system in your engineering team: those working on the 'future' (Rust) and those maintaining the 'past' (current system), which destroys team cohesion and knowledge sharing.
The economic analysis ignores the compounding effect of delayed features — each month of rewrite delays all future feature development, creating exponential opportunity cost rather than linear.

Rust's learning curve means your most experienced engineers will be least productive initially, while junior engineers may adapt faster but lack architectural judgment, creating inverted competence during critical design phases.

candidates.content: The decision to rewrite a backend in Rust isn't primarily about technical superiority—it's about system dynamics. You're proposing to replace one complex adaptive system (your current backend ecosystem) with another. The critical question is whether the emergent properties of the Rust-based system will create positive feedback loops that outweigh the inevitable disruption. Consider second-order effects: A Rust rewrite will likely reduce runtime memory errors, but it may increase development-time cognitive load, potentially slowing feature velocity for months. This creates a hidden cost—while you're rewriting, competitors aren't. Third-order effects: The 'rewrite culture' you establish becomes part of your organizational DNA. Future teams may default to rewrites rather than incremental improvements, creating a cycle of perpetual disruption. The economic analysis (SP3) must model not just direct costs but how the rewrite affects your system's ability to adapt. If Rust's compile-time guarantees reduce debugging time by 30%, that's not just cost savings—it changes how your team allocates cognitive resources, potentially enabling more ambitious features. However, if your team struggles with Rust's learning curve, you might experience a 'productivity valley' lasting 6-12 months where both maintenance of the old system and development of the new system suffer simultaneously. The most dangerous assumption is that architecture translates directly. Systems evolve to fit their implementation language's idioms. A Python backend likely uses dynamic patterns that don't map cleanly to Rust's static world, forcing either architectural compromises or complete redesign.
key_insights: The rewrite decision creates a meta-system: Your choice establishes organizational patterns that will influence future technical decisions far beyond this specific backend.

Rust's safety guarantees shift failure modes rather than eliminate them: You'll trade runtime null pointer exceptions for development-time borrow checker struggles and potentially more complex deployment pipelines.

The greatest risk isn't technical failure but ecosystem fragmentation: During the transition, you'll maintain two systems, creating knowledge silos and increasing bus factor vulnerability until the old system is fully decommissioned.

tests.failure_mode: Solution collapses under its own complexity. Rust's strict safety guarantees and compile-time checks create massive friction during development. The team lacks sufficient Rust expertise, leading to months of unproductive struggle. Memory safety comes at the cost of development velocity. The rewrite never reaches production parity with the existing system, consuming all engineering resources while delivering zero business value.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Immediately halt the Rust rewrite. 2. Revert to the existing backend. 3. Conduct a post-mortem to document why 'rewrite from scratch' was a catastrophic decision. 4. Implement incremental improvements to the current system using proven technologies the team already masters.

tests.failure_mode: Core assumptions about team capacity, timeline, and system complexity shatter. Key developers leave the company. The business demands new features that cannot wait for a multi-year rewrite. The Rust codebase becomes a sprawling, half-finished monument that nobody fully understands. Integration with existing services fails due to incompatible data models and error handling. The project is officially canceled after burning 18 months of engineering budget.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Declare the rewrite project dead. 2. Freeze the Rust repository. 3. Reassign all engineers to maintain and incrementally refactor the original system. 4. If critical functionality was accidentally moved to the new system, plan a high-risk, one-time migration back to the old codebase, accepting data loss and downtime.

tests.failure_mode: An intelligent adversary (e.g., a competing team, a malicious actor, or simply the relentless pressure of the market) exploits the rewrite's vulnerability: it delivers no value while consuming all resources. They ship competing features rapidly using agile methods on the old stack or a new microservice. They lobby management that the Rust team is blocking progress. The rewrite team is disbanded, their work discarded, and the company's technical debt is now higher due to the wasted effort and demoralized engineers.
tests.recovery_path: 1. There is no recovery for the Rust rewrite itself; it is a total loss. 2. The organization must recover by dissolving the rewrite team. 3. Salvage any useful libraries or algorithms from the Rust code, if they exist. 4. Mandate that all future projects must deliver customer-visible value in production within one quarter.
